elements.sent-2411-001: It is generally accepted that radio relics are the result of synchrotron emission from shock-accelerated electrons.
elements.sent-2411-002: Current models, however, are still unable to explain several aspects of their formation.
elements.sent-2411-003: In this paper, we focus on three outstanding problems: i) Mach number estimates derived from radio data do not agree with those derived from X-ray data, ii) cooling length arguments imply a magnetic field that is at least an order of magnitude larger than the surrounding intracluster medium (ICM), and iii) spectral index variations do not agree with standard cooling models.
elements.sent-2411-004: We use a hybrid approach to solve these problems; first identifying typical shock conditions in cosmological simulations and then using these to inform idealized shock-tube simulations, which can be run with substantially higher resolution.
elements.sent-2411-005: We post-process our simulations with the cosmic ray electron spectra code CREST and the emission code CRAYON+, allowing us to generate mock observables ab-initio.
elements.sent-2411-006: We identify that upon running into an accretion shock, merger shocks generate a dense, shock-compressed sheet, which, in turn, runs into upstream density fluctuations in pressure equilibrium.
elements.sent-2411-007: This mechanism directly gives rise to solutions to the three aforementioned problems: density fluctuations lead to a distribution of Mach numbers forming at the shock-front.

elements.sent-2411-014: Radio relics have presented a problem for theorists ever since \citet{mills1960 reported an unusually strong spectral flux density of 57 $\upmu$Jy in the vicinity of Abell S753\footnote{This source is known today as 1401-33.. .}}

elements.sent-2411-016: Today, these are recognized as a radio halo and a radio relic\footnote{The radio relics referred to in this paper have, for historical reasons, variously been called radio gischts \citep{kempner2004 , giant radio relics \citep{pinzke2013 , and cluster radio shocks \citep{vanweeren2019 .. , respectively.}}}}

elements.sent-2411-085: All simulations presented in this paper have been run with the moving-mesh code Arepo \citep{springel2010, pakmor2016, weinberger2020 .}

elements.subsection-2411-010: Breaking of the laminar flow assumption
elements.sent-2411-415: It is frequently assumed that distance from the shock front is a reliable measure for time cooled \citep[see, e.g.][]{vanweeren2012, rajpurohit2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-416: We now investigate whether this assumption is valid, particularly in light of the turbulence we identified in Sect.~\ref{sec:RT .}
elements.sent-2411-417: For this purpose, we reuse the time bins just discussed, making histograms of the distance from the shock median for each binned population.
elements.sent-2411-418: These histograms are presented in the bottom row of Fig.~\ref{figure:spectra-binned-by-tinj .}
elements.sent-2411-419: The gray shaded regions indicate data from the Flat simulations.
elements.sent-2411-420: It can be seen that whilst there is some overlap between time bins, owing to the aforementioned magnetic pressure fluctuations, the overlap is relatively minimal.
elements.sent-2411-421: This indicates that the flow is predominantly laminar.
elements.sent-2411-422: In contrast, tracers from the Turb simulations, represented by the blue shaded regions, overlap strongly.
elements.sent-2411-423: Indeed, there are tracers in the last time bin that are still close to the shock front.
elements.sent-2411-424: Two further aspects further distinguish the Turb simulations from their Flat counterparts: firstly, there are well-populated regions of negative distance due to the corrugation of the shock front (see Sec~\ref{sec:RT ), and secondly, the maximum distance from the shock is substantially larger in the Turb runs.}
elements.sent-2411-425: Indeed, the relative maximum distance increases proportionally with the strength of the shock: tracers reach 32\% and 67\% further in Mach 2 and Mach 3 shocks, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-426: This comes about primarily due to the inertia of the high-density fluctuations; higher density clumps experience less acceleration and can therefore better penetrate downstream.
elements.sent-2411-427: Higher Mach number shocks, meanwhile, have faster speeds, resulting in a greater distance opening up between the shock front and such clumps.
elements.sent-2411-428: This effect is further reinforced by the Rayleigh-Taylor instability as analyzed in Sect.~\ref{sec:RT , which itself is more effective at faster shock speeds, when the downstream is more dynamic.}
elements.sent-2411-429: The consequence of all of these effects is that, once turbulent density fluctuations are taken into account, distance from the shock front is no longer a good indicator for time since injection for individual electrons.

elements.sent-2411-588: Color-color diagrams are often used in radio observations to understand the underlying acceleration and cooling processes.

elements.sent-2411-590: Points on this plane then measure the spectral curvature, i.e., the functional behavior of $\rmn{d}\log_{10 j_\nu / \rmn{d}\log_{10}\nu$ in the given frequency range, which, in turn, is a measure of the spectral shape.}

elements.sent-2411-592: This can then be compared against the trajectories produced by various models.
elements.sent-2411-593: As discussed in Sect.~\ref{sec:intro , four models are typically used.}
elements.sent-2411-594: These are abbreviated: KP, JP, CI, and KGJP\footnote{Examples of the characteristic spectral shapes formed by each model can be seen in figure~11 of \citet{vanweeren2012 .. .}}

elements.sent-2411-598: In contrast, the JP model produces homogeneous populations along the line of sight, generating relatively straight lines in the color-color plane, indicative of constant spectral curvature.

elements.subsection-2411-013: What affects the formation of the Rayleigh-Taylor instability?
elements.sent-2411-654: The Rayleigh-Taylor instability has played a large role in the results shown in this paper.
elements.sent-2411-655: Specifically, in Sect.~\ref{sec:magnetic-field we showed that it plays a fundamental role in amplifying the magnetic field through compression and turbulence, whilst in Sect.~\ref{sec:synchrotron-emission we showed that it breaks the commonly used assumption that $d = v_\rmn{post t$, thereby helping to explain observed trends in spectral features.}}}
elements.sent-2411-656: Accordingly, it is important to understand under what circumstances the instability forms.
elements.sent-2411-657: As explained in Sect.~\ref{sec:RT , in order to generate a Rayleigh-Taylor instability, we require the gradients of density and pressure to be misaligned with one another.}
elements.sent-2411-658: Specifically, they must be misaligned such that the induced vorticity amplifies the perturbation.
elements.sent-2411-659: In our case, this means that the density gradient must point across the contact discontinuity towards the shock-compressed region.
elements.sent-2411-660: Furthermore, the amount of vorticity generated is proportional to the size of the gradients.
elements.sent-2411-661: Indeed, the growth rate of the instability follows: \begin{equation}\sigma = \sqrt{|\mathbf{a}| \left( \frac{\rho_2 - \rho_1}{\rho_2 + \rho_1} \right) k}, \label{eq:RTI-growth-rate}\end{equation} where $\mathbf{a \propto -\bnabla P$ is the acceleration of the lighter material into the denser material, the density terms in brackets constitute the Atwood number, and $k$ is the wave number of the perturbation \citep{chandrasekhar1961 .}}
elements.sent-2411-662: In the scenarios presented in this paper, the most influential variable in this equation is the density, where $\rho_2$ represents the density in the shock-compressed region, and $\rho_1$ is the density of the downstream gas on the other side of the contact discontinuity.
elements.sent-2411-663: This difference between these variables is amplified in our simulations due to the rarefaction wave produced (see Fig.~\ref{figure:1D-shock-tube-profiles ).}
elements.sent-2411-664: This wave exists in cosmological simulations as well, as can be seen in the \href{https://youtu.be/Ka-Odrwwamo {movie of Fig.~\ref{figure:cosmological , but is somewhat masked by the overall radial density gradient found in the cluster.}}}
elements.sent-2411-665: We would therefore expect a slightly slower growth of the instability in the cluster; for example, if all other variables remained the same, but $\rho_1$ increased to $1\times 10^{-28}$~g~cm$^{-3}$, we would expect the growth rate to be halved.
elements.sent-2411-666: Slower shocks will also reduce the growth rate, as this affects $a$ in Eq.~\eqref{eq:RTI-growth-rate .}
elements.sent-2411-667: These can be generated by increasing the upstream average density or weakening the Mach number.
elements.sent-2411-668: Indeed, we have predominantly shown results from our Mach 3 simulations throughout this paper, as the growth rate here is 1.5 times faster than in the corresponding Mach 2 simulation.
elements.sent-2411-669: The turbulence is subsequently better developed in these simulations at the end of the 250 Myr runtime.
elements.sent-2411-670: Nonetheless, as we showed in Fig.~\ref{figure:projected-B-histograms , even the Mach 2 simulations are able to produce sufficient turbulence to generate $\upmu$G strength magnetic fields.}
elements.sent-2411-671: The instability will be totally suppressed when $\rho_1 > \rho_2$; i.e. when the density achieved due to shock compression is lower than the density downstream.
elements.sent-2411-672: As can be seen in the \href{https://youtu.be/Ka-Odrwwamo {movie of Fig.~\ref{figure:cosmological , this is the case for the propagating merger shock wave during the initial stages of the cluster merger, before it reaches an accretion shock.}}}
elements.sent-2411-673: Along with geometric and shock-dissipated energy arguments \citep{vazza2012 , as well as the density argument given above, this may help explain why radio relics are so rarely observed close to the cluster center: the mechanism presented in this paper requires conditions only available at the cluster outskirts.}
elements.subsection-2411-014: How does our method for generating turbulence impact the results?
elements.sent-2411-674: Many authors have used shock-tubes to probe resolution-dependent physics.
elements.sent-2411-675: In particular, they have frequently been used to investigate how upstream turbulence affects downstream magnetic field amplification \citep[see, e.g.,][]{ji2016, hu2022, hew2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-676: Few papers, however, have focused on the gas and shock properties relevant to radio relics, with the notable exception of \citet{dominguez-fernandez2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-677: In contrast to our own work, the authors of this paper used the Ornstein–Uhlenbeck method to generate upstream fluctuations.
elements.sent-2411-678: This process creates density, velocity, and magnetic turbulence simultaneously \citep[see][and references therein]{federrath2010 .}
elements.sent-2411-679: The turbulence then decays over time as the shock progresses through it.
elements.sent-2411-680: The key advantage of the Ornstein–Uhlenbeck method is that turbulent properties are self-consistently modeled.
elements.sent-2411-681: Hence fluctuations are correlated, unlike in the simulations we present.
elements.sent-2411-682: Moreover, the magnetic field will display intermittency, which is not true of the Gaussian-random fields used in our initial conditions.
elements.sent-2411-683: Such intermittency is produced naturally by small-scale dynamos \citep{sur2021 , which are expected to generate the magnetic field in the ICM \citep{tevlin2024 .}}
elements.sent-2411-684: Although density turbulence dominates the effects shown in this paper, magnetic fluctuations could be influential for the striations analyzed in Sect.~\ref{sec:synchrotron-emission .}
elements.sent-2411-685: We will investigate this effect in a future study.
elements.sent-2411-686: As already stated, in our own simulations, there is no initial velocity turbulence (see Sect.~\ref{subsec:generating_turb ).}
elements.sent-2411-687: Such turbulence should exist in the ICM, however, which will also result in Mach number variations.
elements.sent-2411-688: The velocity fluctuations would need to be a substantial fraction of the shock speed, however, before they could outweigh the effect of the density fluctuations shown in this paper.
elements.sent-2411-689: We leave a precise parameter study of this effect to future work as well.
elements.sent-2411-690: A standard theory of radio relics has emerged in the past two decades.
elements.sent-2411-691: This states that radio relics are formed by the Mpc-size shocks generated during cluster mergers and that such shocks (re-)accelerate electrons, enabling them to emit in the radio band.
elements.sent-2411-692: This framework has been largely successful in explaining observational data, however, several significant challenges remain.
elements.sent-2411-693: This study has focused on three of those problems:.
elements.sent-2411-694: What is the origin of the $\mathcal{M _\rmn{radio - \mathcal{M _\rmn{X-ray}$ discrepancy if both measurement methods trace the same physical shock?.}}}
elements.sent-2411-695: How do magnetic fields reach $\upmu$G strength in radio relics (as inferred from cooling length arguments) if the surrounding ICM has field strengths an order of magnitude smaller?.
elements.sent-2411-696: Why do spectral variations fail to match standard cooling models in color-color diagrams?.
elements.sent-2411-697: To answer these problems, we have taken a hybrid approach, first identifying typical conditions in cosmological zoom-in simulations of cluster mergers before applying our findings to significantly higher-resolution idealized shock-tubes.
elements.sent-2411-698: In this last step, we use the CR electron spectral code Crest and the emission code Crayon+, thereby producing synchrotron data ab-initio.
elements.sent-2411-699: From our cosmological simulations, we have identified that the merger shock typically meets an accretion shock at distances commonly recorded for radio relics.
elements.sent-2411-700: This leads to the production of a narrow, shock-compressed density sheet (Fig.~\ref{figure:cosmological ).}
elements.sent-2411-701: This scenario can be modeled as a shock-tube problem (see Sect.~\ref{subsec:shock-tube-setup ).}
elements.sent-2411-702: We additionally include upstream density turbulence in our shock-tubes, which is not well-resolved at the outskirts of our zoom-in simulations, but evident in many other previous studies and observations.
elements.sent-2411-703: We choose a log-normal distribution and set the variance of the distribution according to results given in \citet{zhuravleva2013 .}
elements.sent-2411-704: We find that density fluctuations are directly responsible for the following effects:.
elements.sent-2411-705: A Mach number distribution forming at the shock front (Figs.~\ref{figure:shock-tube and~\ref{figure:mach-no-pdf ),.}}
elements.sent-2411-706: A Rayleigh-Taylor instability forming at the contact discontinuity (Figs.~\ref{figure:shock-tube and~\ref{figure:RTI-schematic ), and.}}
elements.sent-2411-708: We show how these features form in Sect.~\ref{sec:RT .}
elements.sent-2411-709: These effects provide answers to our three main questions:.
elements.sent-2411-710: In particular, it flattens the integrated injected spectra and results in the slope at radio emitting frequencies being shallower than the theoretical value of $\alpha_e - 1$ (Fig.~\ref{figure:spectra-binned-by-tinj ).}
elements.sent-2411-711: Both of these effects lead to an over-estimation of the Mach number with respect to the actual peak of the distribution (Table~\ref{tab:inferred_mach_no ).}
elements.sent-2411-712: The effect is particularly strong in weak shocks ($\mathcal{M \lesssim 2$).}
elements.sent-2411-713: This boosts the magnetic field strength from ICM-like conditions up to $\upmu$G levels, although the volume-average remains significantly below this (Figs.~\ref{figure:slice-downstream and~\ref{figure:plasma-beta-slice ).}}
elements.sent-2411-714: Amplification is predominantly driven by the additional adiabatic compression that results from the instability.
elements.sent-2411-715: However, there is also evidence for the existence of a small-scale dynamo in our simulations.
elements.sent-2411-716: Indeed, the peak magnetic field values cannot be reached without it (Fig.~\ref{figure:rho-B-phase-diagram ).}
elements.sent-2411-717: Additionally, we find that the projected synchrotron emission is dominated by the strongest magnetic field values encountered along the line-of-sight.
elements.sent-2411-718: This means that estimations of the magnetic field made from emission are heavily biased by these regions; values inferred from cooling length arguments are only representative of the tail of the distribution, not the volume-average (Figs.~\ref{figure:t_inj-and-B-field-projections and~\ref{figure:projected-B-histograms ).}}
elements.sent-2411-719: This is especially true on an electron-by-electron basis, where distance from the shock front is a poor indicator of time since injection (Figs.~\ref{figure:spectra-binned-by-tinj and~\ref{figure:slice-downstream ).}}
elements.sent-2411-720: The relationship is somewhat recovered when projecting the synchrotron-weighted time since injection, but even here $d = v_\rmn{post t$ no longer holds true (Fig.~\ref{figure:t_inj-and-B-field-projections .}}
elements.sent-2411-721: Turbulence leads to the mixing of spectra along the line-of-sight, which acts to flatten the spectral curvature and explains why standard cooling models do not work for radio relics (Figs.~\ref{figure:color-color and~\ref{figure:color-color-schematic ).}}
elements.sent-2411-722: In addition, we find that features in surface brightness and spectral index maps can be attributed to specific mechanisms (Fig.\ref{figure:intensity-and-spectral-index ).}
elements.sent-2411-723: Specifically, the corrugation of the shock front leads to filamentary emission in projection.
